content.assessment: This proposal is the counterfactual baseline in which demographic decline and statutory class-size minimums mechanically force network consolidation absent policy action. It is the proposal most directly grounded in network-planning dynamics, since it relies entirely on capacity, transport, and viability constraints operating through minimum class-size enforcement and regional population contraction. The evidence indicates this passive process will be geographically uneven, disproportionately affecting rural provision (where fragile rural secondary schools already face closure risk on weak evidence) while urban flagship schools may retain oversubscription, meaning network resilience is not preserved uniformly.
opportunities: Demographic contraction could be leveraged as political cover for a more deliberate, planned consolidation (as proposed in TP-live-t2) rather than left as an uncoordinated passive process
strengths: Directly grounded in verifiable capacity mechanics: statutory minimum class-size thresholds becoming unenforceable in shrinking rural/low-density regions provides a concrete, quantifiable network-consolidation pathway
Regional child-population contraction data (10-30% declines in specific regions) offers a strong evidentiary basis for anticipating where network viability challenges will concentrate
threats: Church/private schools operating outside the demographic-driven state consolidation pressure could see a relatively increased share as public provision contracts, altering sector composition in ways outside public network-planning control

Uneven geographic impact of passive contraction could deepen rather than narrow rural-urban access disparities, undermining overall network resilience even as aggregate selective-track share declines nationally
weaknesses: Passive contraction does not correct, and may worsen, existing geographic stratification, since urban flagship schools with path-dependent prestige are likely to retain oversubscription and selectivity even as rural provision withers

Relies on weak evidence (a single, unaudited interview) regarding settlement-viability consequences of rural secondary school closure, so downstream network-resilience claims at the settlement level remain uncertain
